title: Module 4 quiz: Webhooks and event-driven integration

questions.prompt: Your webhook handler runs heavy DB writes inline. P99 is 45s. Stripe times out half your webhooks. Fix?

choices.text: Acknowledge fast, process async. Verify signature → enqueue → return 200. Background worker does heavy work. Stripe times out at ~30s; quick ack prevents retry storms.

questions.explanation: Canonical pattern: verify + enqueue + ack. Heavy work runs in background; handler is sub-second.

questions.prompt: Your webhook signatures started failing after a framework upgrade. Most likely cause?

choices.text: Middleware parses JSON before your handler, modifying raw bytes. Signature is HMAC over raw body — any reformatting breaks verification. Preserve raw body on webhook route (express.raw / request.text()) before stripe.webhooks.constructEvent.

questions.explanation: Raw-body bug is the #1 cause of mysterious signature failures. Preserve raw bytes on the webhook route specifically.

questions.prompt: Your handler assumes events arrive in order: created → invoice → paid. Sometimes paid arrives first; handler errors. Fix?

choices.text: Tell Stripe to deliver in order.
Refactor to upsert based on the authoritative state in the event payload, not a state machine your code imposes. Stripe doesn't guarantee order; build order-independent handlers.

questions.explanation: Stripe is at-least-once and not strictly ordered. Upsert by event payload; refetch from Stripe for canonical state when stakes warrant.

questions.prompt: Why dedupe by event.id in your webhook handler?

choices.text: Stripe is at-least-once: same event may deliver multiple times due to retries, network blips, redeliveries. Without dedup, duplicate side effects (duplicate emails, double grants, duplicate metric inserts). Store event IDs with unique constraint; skip on duplicate.

questions.explanation: At-least-once delivery makes idempotency mandatory. Atomic check + insert on event.id with unique index; subsequent deliveries skip.

questions.prompt: For a subscription SaaS, what's the right webhook subscription strategy?

choices.text: Subscribe to ~13 specific event types covering subscription lifecycle (created, updated, deleted, trial_will_end), invoice lifecycle (paid, payment_failed, payment_action_required), payments (intent succeeded/failed), refunds, disputes. Pair with daily reconciliation job. Specific events = cleaner logs, faster handlers, lower cost.

questions.explanation: Selective event subscription beats firehose. Webhooks for immediate sync; reconciliation for drift recovery. Together they're robust.
